That's Senator Barbara Boxer, Democrat of California, one of the sponsors of the Airline Passenger Bill of Rights Act.
This week, the World Health Organization declared an official end to an epidemic of the Marburg virus in Angola.
It was the largest outbreak of Marburg ever and claimed as many as 329 lives.
Its victims died horrific deaths, suffering high fever, massive bleeding and multiple organ failure.
International health workers quickly learned the local culture made it hard to contain the outbreak.
Julienne Anoko is an anthropologist living in Angola.
She worked with the WHO and says traditional mourning rituals require that a dead body be laid out and washed by loved ones.
They wash the body and the family's members and the friends have to embrace him, kiss him.
They wash and kiss and cry and dance and so on.
And you know that the most important way of contamination is contact.
At the same time, Angolans became suspicious of hospitals.
Sick people would go there and die rather than return home well.
So Rice focuses on steps her patients can take to cope with the consequences of heat waves and more tiny particles floating into Heafy's lungs.

And you know, it - so what they're studying - so I could gush about the animals for a long time, but there's actual science going on here.
And the idea is to understand sort of how muscles work and how our skeletons help us move.
And engineers are collaborating with them to make better robots, and they have sort of influence on people looking for treatments for movement disorders in people.

I mean, this is a really basic biology happening here, but you need to have all these kinds of animals to compare how a guinea fowl runs versus how an emu runs or - and so they also have, you know, really cool gadgets.
So there's like a huge X-ray machine that takes X-ray video and high-speed cameras everywhere.

This is News & Notes.
I'm Farai Chideya.
On today's Roundtable, blacks in New York protest against police brutality and New Jersey lawmaker's approve civil unions in their state.
Joining us now from New York to discuss these topics are Robert George, an editorial writer at the New York Post, and Marcelo Suarez-Orozco, professor of immigration studies at NYU.
In Boston, we've got Callie Crossley, social and cultural commentator on the local TV show "Beat the Press.
I want to thank you all for being with us and go to the Sean Bell rally in New York City.
Sean Bell was the man who was shot in New York by police officers.
It's revived a very difficult relationship between the police and black New York residents.
Now he was a groom-to-be right before his wedding day, and it's compared to Amadou Diallo, who was also shot by police on his doorstep.
So on Saturday there was a march for justice against police brutality held in the city.
Here's what one participant had to say.
These issues never happen in other communities.
You never hear about a white man, an unarmed white man being shot, you know, 40, 50 times.
It never occurs.
And we need to stop it.
Callie, what's the effect of these rallies?
Well, I think that there is always power in acknowledgement.
Now, you know, so there's a couple of ways to look at effect, I guess.
The bottom line effect would be, of course, the rally occurs and then something changes in city hall, or the police chiefs sit down and talk with community leaders.
That's the best of all possible scenarios.
But the second part of that I think is equally important, and that is for the community, however small its voice may be, to say we know that this happened and this is not right.

And we're going to continue to raise the issue until we can at least get other citizens in New York to agree that this is not something they would want to have in their communities, and it needs to be addressed.
So I think there's a two-fold effect, and I think that it's powerful to demand acknowledgement of something that's happened that is just not right in the community.
Marcelo, you're also in the New York community, the greater New York area.
You know, there's been a lot of discussion about whether or not marches and rallies have the same potency they used to.
In a media-saturated world, people are going to see this, but will they heed the call?
Is there anything else that's an option?
Well, surely there are multiple options here, but I think a completely agree with Callie here that the issue is one of acknowledgment.
Look, this is a wound in the city.
Fifty shots were fired.
We need transparency.
We need to understand clearly what happened and what concrete steps are being taken to prevent this from happening again.
And it is through transparency.
It is through dialogue.
It is really through the demand for justice that these issues ultimately get addressed.
So I think that marches is one way of acknowledging when wrongs need to be righted.
There are many, many other ways.
You mentioned the Internet.
You mentioned media.
You mentioned there are many, many ways in which citizens can make their voices heard.
Robert, what's your paper saying about this?

That second sentence--it's so musical.
Oh, yeah.
It has its intonation that is, that is always natural.
